brief.title: Plastiq
brief.scope: Contributed to the customer-facing payment platform, building and improving web experiences that enabled businesses to pay vendors using credit cards, even when those vendors did not directly accept card payments. The work focused on creating intuitive, reliable, and performant user interfaces for complex financial workflows.
brief.problem: Business payments involve multiple validation rules, funding sources, payment methods, and compliance requirements. The challenge was to deliver a user experience that simplified these workflows while accurately reflecting the complexity of the underlying financial system.
key_challenges: Designing interfaces that simplified complex payment workflows without hiding critical financial information.
Coordinating frontend behavior with evolving backend APIs and business rules.
Maintaining a responsive and reliable experience for users managing time-sensitive financial transactions.
key_contributions: Developed and enhanced customer-facing features for the web application using a reusable component library built with React, MUI, and Styled Components.
Collaborated closely with Product and Design to translate business requirements into intuitive user experiences, contributing to the team's Design System.
Worked with backend engineers to integrate GraphQL (via Apollo Client) and microservice-based payment APIs, ensuring consistent handling of validation, errors, and transaction states.
Mentored teammates and contributed to Agile ceremonies, supporting team-level technical decisions.
technical_focus: API integration and asynchronous data flows (GraphQL, Apollo Client).
Form validation, state management, and responsive user interfaces.
Test coverage across unit, integration, and e2e levels (RTL, Postman, Browserstack).
brief.suggested_diagrams: End-to-end payment workflow. Frontend-to-backend interaction during payment processing. Component architecture for payment forms and transaction management.
suggested_diagrams_charts:
flowchart TD
    A["Business initiates payment"] --> B["Select vendor and invoice"]
    B --> C["Choose funding source"]
    C --> D["Select payment method: Credit Card"]
    D --> E{"Validation passed?"}
    E -- No --> F["Show validation errors"]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G["Process transaction"]
    G --> H{"Vendor accepts cards?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Direct card payment to vendor"]
    H -- No --> J["Plastiq issues alternate payment to vendor"]
    I --> K["Transaction confirmed"]
    J --> K

sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant UI as React Frontend
    participant API as Apollo Client and GraphQL
    participant SVC as Payment Microservice
    participant Vendor

    User->>UI: Submit payment form
    UI->>UI: Validate form fields
    UI->>API: Send payment mutation
    API->>SVC: Forward payment request
    SVC->>SVC: Verify funding source and rules
    SVC-->>API: Transaction status
    API-->>UI: Update transaction state
    UI-->>User: Show confirmation or error
    SVC->>Vendor: Issue payment

flowchart TD
    PF["PaymentForm"] --> FS["FundingSourceSelector"]
    PF --> PM["PaymentMethodSelector"]
    PF --> VE["ValidationEngine"]
    PF --> TS["TransactionSummary"]
    PF --> SB["SubmitButton"]
    PF --> RS[("Redux Store")]
    RS --> TH["TransactionHistory"]
    RS --> TS
system_impact: Improved usability for complex payment scenarios.
Increased consistency across payment-related user interfaces through shared design system components.
Contributed to a more maintainable frontend codebase through reusable components and shared patterns.
learnings: Financial products require balancing usability with precision and transparency. Frontend engineering extends beyond interface implementation; it must accurately represent complex business logic. This experience strengthened my ability to think beyond individual screens and consider how user experience, business rules, and system architecture interact, reinforcing my progression toward product and platform architecture.
